pattern: Reporting the peak overlap after every insertion is a running-maximum sweep over interval start/end deltas, the classic boundary-counting technique.
hints: The largest number of simultaneous events is the highest the count of currently-open intervals ever reaches.
Represent each event as a +1 at its start and a -1 at its end, then the answer is the maximum prefix sum over all boundary points in order.
Keep a sorted multiset of deltas keyed by time; after adding the new event's two deltas, sweep the boundaries accumulating the count and return the largest value seen.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: peak climbs to 3 and stays there
a single event gives a peak of one
touching-only intervals never stack so the peak stays one
fully identical intervals raise the peak by one each time
the peak is the historical maximum and never decreases even when later events are disjoint
nested intervals build up overlap at a shared inner point

stress.description: many random events over a small coordinate space so the peak overlap grows steadily
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    ops = ['MyCalendarThree']
    args = [[]]
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        s = rng.randint(0, 30)
        e = s + rng.randint(1, 12)
        ops.append('book'); args.append([s, e])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: narrow range pushes overlaps high so the running maximum changes often
